q: To be, or not to be, that is the question: whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer the slings and arrows of outrageous fortune, or to take arms against a sea of troubles and by opposing end them.
To die, to sleep — to sleep, perchance to dream. Ay, there's the rub: for in that sleep of death what dreams may come, when we have shuffled off this mortal coil.

—What do you read, my lord?
—Words, words, words.
—What is the matter, my lord?
—Between who?
—I mean the matter that you read, my lord.
—Slanders, sir.
There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio, than are dreamt of in your philosophy.

—Though this be madness, yet there is method in 't.
—Will you walk out of the air, my lord?
—Into my grave?
Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.
Use every man after his desert, and who should 'scape whipping? Use them after your own honour and dignity: the less they deserve, the more merit is in your bounty.

Neither a borrower nor a lender be, for loan oft loses both itself and friend, and borrowing dulls the edge of husbandry. This above all: to thine own self be true, and it must follow, as the night the day, thou canst not then be false to any man.
The rest is silence.

—How does my good Lord Hamlet?
—Well, God-a-mercy.
—Do you know me, my lord?
—Excellent well; you are a fishmonger.
Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player that struts and frets his hour upon the stage and then is heard no more: it is a tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing.
Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day to the last syllable of recorded time, and all our yesterdays have lighted fools the way to dusty death.
Fair is foul, and foul is fair: hover through the fog and filthy air.
Is this a dagger which I see before me, the handle toward my hand? Come, let me clutch thee. I have thee not, and yet I see thee still.
Out, damned spot! Out, I say!
By the pricking of my thumbs, something wicked this way comes. Open, locks, whoever knocks!
Things without all remedy should be without regard: what's done is done.

—O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name; or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, and I'll no longer be a Capulet.
—Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?
What's in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.

—How camest thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, and the place death, considering who thou art, if any of my kinsmen find thee here.
—With love's light wings did I o'er-perch these walls, for stony limits cannot hold love out.

—O, swear not by the moon, the inconstant moon, that monthly changes in her circled orb, lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
—What shall I swear by?
—Do not swear at all; or, if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self.
Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow, that I shall say good night till it be morrow.
These violent delights have violent ends, and in their triumph die, like fire and powder, which as they kiss consume.
We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.
Hell is empty, and all the devils are here!
O, wonder! How many goodly creatures are there here! How beauteous mankind is! O brave new world, that has such people in 't!
What's past is prologue; what to come, in yours and my discharge.
The isle is full of noises, sounds, and sweet airs, that give delight and hurt not. Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments will hum about mine ears.
For aught that I could ever read, could ever hear by tale or history, the course of true love never did run smooth.
Love looks not with the eyes, but with the mind; and therefore is winged Cupid painted blind.

The lunatic, the lover, and the poet are of imagination all compact: one sees more devils than vast hell can hold, that is, the madman; the lover, all as frantic, sees Helen's beauty in a brow of Egypt.
Lord, what fools these mortals be!
O, when she's angry, she is keen and shrewd! She was a vixen when she went to school; and though she be but little, she is fierce.
If we shadows have offended, think but this, and all is mended: that you have but slumbered here while these visions did appear.
O, beware, my lord, of jealousy! It is the green-eyed monster which doth mock the meat it feeds on.

Who steals my purse steals trash; 'tis something, nothing; 'twas mine, 'tis his, and has been slave to thousands. But he that filches from me my good name robs me of that which not enriches him and makes me poor indeed.
Speak of me as I am; nothing extenuate, nor set down aught in malice. Then must you speak of one that loved not wisely, but too well.
Keep up your bright swords, for the dew will rust them.
How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is to have a thankless child!

—Nothing will come of nothing. Speak again.
—Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave my heart into my mouth. I love your majesty according to my bond; no more nor less.
Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! Rage! Blow! You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout till you have drenched our steeples, drowned the cocks!
As flies to wanton boys are we to the gods: they kill us for their sport.
I am a man more sinned against than sinning.
All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players: they have their exits and their entrances, and one man in his time plays many parts.
The fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows himself to be a fool.
No sooner met but they looked, no sooner looked but they loved, no sooner loved but they sighed.

Love is merely a madness, and, I tell you, deserves as well a dark house and a whip as madmen do; and the reason why they are not so punished and cured is that the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love too.
If you prick us, do we not bleed? If you tickle us, do we not laugh? If you poison us, do we not die? And if you wrong us, shall we not revenge?
The quality of mercy is not strained; it droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven upon the place beneath. It is twice blest: it blesseth him that gives and him that takes.
The devil can cite Scripture for his purpose. An evil soul producing holy witness is like a villain with a smiling cheek.
All that glisters is not gold; often have you heard that told. Many a man his life hath sold but my outside to behold.
How far that little candle throws his beams! So shines a good deed in a naughty world.

The man that hath no music in himself, nor is not moved with concord of sweet sounds, is fit for treasons, stratagems, and spoils; the motions of his spirit are dull as night and his affections dark as Erebus. Let no such man be trusted.
Men at some time are masters of their fates: the fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings.

Cowards die many times before their deaths; the valiant never taste of death but once. Of all the wonders that I yet have heard, it seems to me most strange that men should fear, seeing that death, a necessary end, will come when it will come.

—Speak; Caesar is turned to hear.
—Beware the ides of March.
Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears: I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him. The evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interrèd with their bones.
There is a tide in the affairs of men which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune; omitted, all the voyage of their life is bound in shallows and in miseries.
If music be the food of love, play on; give me excess of it, that, surfeiting, the appetite may sicken, and so die.
Be not afraid of greatness: some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon 'em.
Better a witty fool than a foolish wit.
There is no darkness but ignorance, in which thou art more puzzled than the Egyptians in their fog.
A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!
Now is the winter of our discontent made glorious summer by this sun of York, and all the clouds that loured upon our house in the deep bosom of the ocean buried.
And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover to entertain these fair well-spoken days, I am determinèd to prove a villain and hate the idle pleasures of these days.
Conscience is but a word that cowards use, devised at first to keep the strong in awe.

What is life? A frenzy.
What is life? An illusion,
a shadow, a fiction,
and the greatest good is small:
for all of life is a dream,
and dreams themselves are dreams.

Alas, wretched me! Alas, unhappy!
I strive, o heavens, to learn,
since you treat me so,
what crime I committed
against you by being born;
though if I was born, I understand
what crime I have committed:
sufficient cause there has been
for your justice and rigour,
since the greatest crime
of man is to have been born.

They tell of a sage who one day
was so poor and wretched
that he sustained himself only
on some herbs he gathered.
"Can there be another," he said to himself,
"poorer and sadder than I?"
And when he turned his face
he found the answer, seeing
another sage going along gathering
the leaves that he had thrown away.

The bird is born, and with the finery
that gives it supreme beauty,
it is scarcely a feathered flower
or a posy with wings
when it cuts through the ethereal halls
with swiftness,
refusing the shelter
of the nest it leaves in calm;
and I, having more soul,
have less liberty?

But, be it truth or dream,
to do right is what matters:
if it be truth, for truth's sake;
if not, to win us friends
for when we wake.

With any role it is won,
for the whole of human life
is a play of parts.
w: The Great Theatre of the World

q:
—I shall kill you, believe it,
on this rack, you villagers.
Who killed the Comendador?
—Fuenteovejuna, sir.

Cowardly hares you were born;
barbarians you are, not Spaniards.
Hens! You suffer other men
to enjoy your women!
Put distaffs in your belts.
Why do you gird on swords?

By night they killed him,
the knight,
the pride of Medina,
the flower of Olmedo.
Shadows warned him
not to set out,
and counselled him
not to go,
the knight,
the pride of Medina,
the flower of Olmedo.

Doubt it not: she is by nature
the gardener's dog:
she neither eats nor lets eat,
is neither outside nor within.

—You who feign and deceive
women in this fashion
will pay for it in death.
—What long credit you give me!

All Seville calls me
the Trickster, and the greatest
pleasure I can have
is to trick a woman
and leave her without honour.

This is the justice of God:
who does such deeds, so shall he pay.

Ah! Is it not true, angel of love,
that on this secluded shore
the moon shines more purely
and one breathes better?

Down to the cottages I went,
up to the palaces I climbed,
the cloisters' walls I scaled,
and everywhere I left
bitter memory of me.

I called on heaven and it did not hear me,
and since it shuts its gates against me,
let heaven, and not I, answer
for my steps upon the earth.

—I am Doña Inés, Don Juan,
who heard you from her grave.
—Then you live?
—For you;
but I keep my purgatory
in that funeral marble
they carved for me.

—They have fled! They have fled! She and Leonardo. On the horse. They ride embraced, like a gust of wind.
—It is not true! Not my daughter!
—Your daughter, yes! Offshoot of a wicked mother, and he, he too, he. But now she is my son's wife!
—After them! Who has a horse?

Let us go to the dark corner,
where I may love you always,
for I care nothing for people,
nor the poison they cast on us.
The knife, the knife... A curse on them all, and on the scoundrel who invented them.

With a knife,
with a little knife
that barely fits the hand,
but that slides in clean
through astonished flesh
and stops at the place
where trembles, entangled,
the dark root of the scream.

—Let me weep with you.
—Weep, but at the door.
In the eight years this mourning lasts, the wind from the street shall not enter this house. Imagine that we have bricked up the doors and the windows.
Needle and thread for females. Whip and mule for the male. That is the lot of people born with means.
To be born a woman is the greatest punishment.

And I want no weeping. Death must be looked at face to face. Silence! Be quiet, I said! Your tears when you are alone. We shall all drown ourselves in a sea of mourning! She, the youngest daughter of Bernarda Alba, died a virgin. Did you hear me? Silence, silence I said! Silence!
Withered. Withered, but sure. Now I truly know it. And alone.
Every woman has blood for four or five children, and when she does not have them it turns to poison, as it will in me.
Do not come near, for I have killed my child, I myself have killed my child!

—The ultraists are frauds. Esperpentism was invented by Goya. The classical heroes have gone strolling in the Callejón del Gato.
—You are completely drunk!
—The classical heroes reflected in concave mirrors yield the Esperpento. The tragic sense of Spanish life can only be rendered through an aesthetic that is systematically deformed.

—Spain is a grotesque deformation of European civilisation.
—It may be! I take no part.
—The most beautiful images in a concave mirror are absurd.

—Don Latino de Hispalis, grotesque personage, I shall immortalise you in a novel!
—A tragedy, Max.
—Our tragedy is not a tragedy.
—Well, it must be something!
—The Esperpento.
Behold the scaffolding of the old farce, the one that relieved in village inns the weariness of carters, the one that entranced the simple villagers in the squares of humble towns.
I was not born to share hatred, but love.
Many wonders there are, and yet none more wondrous than man: he crosses the grey sea driven by the stormy south wind.

—And yet you dared to overstep these laws?
—It was not Zeus who proclaimed them, nor did Justice who dwells with the gods below lay down such laws among men. Nor did I think your decrees so strong that a mortal could override the unwritten and unfailing laws of the gods.
Wisdom is by far the greatest part of happiness. One must commit no impiety against the gods. The boastful words of the proud are paid with heavy blows, and in old age teach wisdom.
Count no man happy until he has crossed life's border free from pain.
O light, may I look on you now for the last time! I, born of those I should not have been born of, who lived with those I should not have lived with, and killed those I should not have killed.
Not to be born conquers all reasoning; and once one has come into the world, to return as soon as may be whence one came is second best.
Of all creatures that have life and thought, we women are the most wretched: we must buy a husband with a dowry, and so take a master over our body.
They say we live a life without danger at home, while they fight with the spear. Fools! I would rather stand three times in the battle line than give birth once.
I understand the crime I am about to commit, but my passion is stronger than my deliberations.
My tongue swore, but my heart did not.

—And what remedy did you find for that sickness?
—I lodged blind hopes within mortals.
—A great benefit you gave them with that gift.
—And besides, I gave them fire.
In a word: I hate all the gods who, having received good from me, wrongfully mistreat me.
Time, as it grows old, teaches all things.
Zeus set mortals on the road to understanding when he laid down as sovereign law: by suffering we learn.

—What sensible or glorious thing could we women do, we who sit at home in our rouge and saffron gowns?
—Why, that is exactly what I am counting on to save us!
There is no beast so untamable as woman, nor fire, nor any panther so shameless.
Brekekekex, koax, koax! Brekekekex, koax, koax! Marsh children of the springs, let us sound the hymn that goes with the flutes, my resonant song.
I am a man: I consider nothing human alien to me.
Man is a wolf to man, and no man, when he knows not what the other is.
Now I am Medea: my genius has grown through evils.

—What?
—Cover up that bosom, which I can't endure to look on. Things like that offend our souls, and fill our minds with sinful thoughts.
It is the public scandal that constitutes the offence: to sin in private is not to sin at all.
Thieves! thieves! assassins! murder! Justice, just heavens! I am undone; I am murdered; they have cut my throat; they have stolen my money!

—One must eat to live, and not live to eat.
—How well said that is!
Nearly all men die of their remedies, and not of their illnesses.
The more we love someone, the less we should flatter them: pure love shows itself in pardoning nothing.

—Before all else you are a wife and a mother.
—I no longer believe that. Before all else I am a human being, just as much as you are... or at least I must try to become one.

—Are they not your duties to your husband and your children?
—I have other duties just as sacred.
—That you have not. What duties could those be?
—Duties to myself.

—Shot herself! Shot herself in the temple! Fancy that!
—Good God! People don't do such things.

It is not only what we have inherited from our father and mother that walks in us. It is all sorts of dead ideas, and lifeless old beliefs, and so forth. They have no vitality, but they cling to us all the same, and we cannot shake them off.

—Why do you always wear black?
—I am in mourning for my life. I am unhappy.
I am a seagull... No, that's not it. I am an actress. Yes, that's it!
New forms are needed. New forms are needed, and if there are none, then better nothing at all.

Now I know, I understand, that in our work — acting or writing, it's all the same — the main thing is not fame, not glory, not what I used to dream of, but knowing how to endure. Know how to bear your cross, and have faith.
What can we do? We must live! We shall live, Uncle Vanya. We shall live through a long, long line of days, of long evenings.

We shall rest. We shall hear the angels, we shall see the whole sky all sparkling with diamonds, we shall see how all earthly evil and all our sufferings drown in the mercy that will fill the whole world.
Everything in a human being should be beautiful: the face, the clothes, the soul, the thoughts.
All Russia is our orchard. The land is great and beautiful, there are many marvellous places in it.
My dear, my tender, my beautiful orchard! My life, my youth, my happiness, farewell! Farewell!

—Good-bye, home! Good-bye, old life!
—Welcome, new life!
To Moscow! To Moscow! To Moscow!

Time will pass on, and we shall depart for ever, we shall be forgotten; they will forget our faces, voices, and even how many there were of us, but our sufferings will turn into joy for those who will live after us.
If only we knew, if only we knew!
Grey, dear friend, is all theory, and green the golden tree of life.
If ever I say to the passing moment: "Stay, thou art so fair!" — then you may cast me into chains, then I will gladly accept my ruin.

—Who are you, then?
—I am part of that power which eternally wills evil and eternally works good.
I am the spirit that eternally denies. And rightly so, for all that comes to be deserves to perish.
It is written: "In the beginning was the Word"... Here I stop already! Who helps me on?... and I write confidently: "In the beginning was the Deed".

Friendly: "When you sup it must annoy you, dipping in your cup; you need a drinking-bowl of special shape!" Descriptive: "'Tis a rock!... a peak!... a cape! A cape, forsooth! 'Tis a peninsular!"

—Ah, what a fascinating Puritan you are, Lady Windermere!
—The adjective was unnecessary, Lord Darlington.
—I couldn't help it. I can resist everything except temptation.

—As a matter of fact... we have come here in search of an author...
—An author? What author?
—Any author, sir.
—But there's no author here. We are not rehearsing a new piece.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q: When a character is born, he acquires at once such an independence, even of his own author, that he can be imagined by everybody even in many other situations where the author never dreamed of placing him; and so he acquires for himself a meaning which the author never thought of giving him.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q: Life is full of infinite absurdities, which, strangely enough, do not even need to appear plausible, since they are true.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q:
—The truth? The truth is simply this. I am the daughter of Signora Frola, and I am the second wife of Signor Ponza. Yes, and—for myself, I am nobody, I am nobody...
—Ah, but no, madam, for yourself... you must be... either the one or the other.
—Not at all, not at all, sir! No, for myself I am... whoever you choose to have me.
w: Right You Are (If You Think So)

q: I find myself seated at the top of a high pillar and I see no possible way to get down. I grow dizzy when I look down, but down I must. But I'm not brave enough to throw myself; I cannot hold fast and I long to fall—but I don't fall.

My wound is not so recent. Scarcely had I been bound to Theseus by the marriage yoke, and happiness and peace seem'd well secured, when Athens show'd me my proud enemy. I look'd, alternately turn'd pale and blush'd to see him, and my soul grew all distraught.

—What about hanging ourselves?
—Hmm. It'd give us an erection.
—An erection!
—With all that follows. Where it falls mandrakes grow. That's why they shriek when you pull them up. Did you not know that?
—Let's hang ourselves immediately!

—Let's go.
—We can't.
—Why not?
—We're waiting for Godot.
—Ah!
The tears of the world are a constant quantity. For each one who begins to weep, somewhere else another stops. The same is true of the laugh.
We are all born mad. Some remain so.

—What do we do now?
—Wait.
—Yes, but while waiting.
—What about hanging ourselves?

—How comes it, then, that thou art out of hell?
—Why, this is hell, nor am I out of it: think'st thou that I, who saw the face of God, and tasted the eternal joys of heaven, am not tormented with ten thousand hells, in being depriv'd of everlasting bliss?

I am a force that moves onward! Blind and deaf agent of funereal mysteries, a soul of misfortune made of darkness.

I shall always be a flower girl to Professor Higgins, because he always treats me as a flower girl, and always will; but I know I can be a lady to you, because you always treat me as a lady, and always will.

First comes the feeding, then comes morality. First the poor must be able to cut their slice from the great loaf of bread.

—Unhappy is the land that has no heroes!
—No: unhappy is the land that needs heroes.

I believe the only purpose of science is to ease the hardship of human existence.

War is nothing but business, and instead of cheese it's lead.
